sub_problems: How can we predict, before issuing an RLHF gradient update in one direction, the magnitude of asymmetric softening that the model will experience on near-boundary prompts in the opposite direction?

What internal-state correlate (residual-stream activation summary, layer-wise gradient norm, prompt-embedding cluster centroid drift) functions as the 'back stress' that accumulates from prior training pushes and governs reverse-direction yield?
How should cyclic RLHF schedules (safety push → helpfulness push → safety push) be paced to avoid Bauschinger-style cumulative asymmetric softening that destabilizes both directions?

How does the asymmetric-softening magnitude scale with the magnitude of the forward training push (linearly? saturating? threshold-bound?), and what empirical tests would distinguish these scaling laws?
